Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty flexible. I'd usually block out about three hours a day across the week, but I wasn't super rigid about when exactly I'd sit down. Being a visual learner, I liked to create diagrams and mind maps from the lecture content, so a lot of my study time went into transforming information into formats that worked better for my brain. I found that spreading my 21 hours across the week kept things manageable without feeling overwhelming.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied quite a bit. I'd have a loose schedule in mind, but if I had extracurriculars or social stuff going on, I'd shift things around. Deadlines would sometimes make me cluster my study sessions, but generally I tried to stay ahead enough that I wasn't cramming. The flexibility worked for me because I could adapt to whatever else was happening in my life.
Q3: Usually I'd log in to check if new materials had been posted or to see discussion threads. I'm pretty social academically, so I liked seeing what my peers were talking about and contributing to those conversations. Sometimes I'd log in just to grab a video or PDF and then work with it offline. The notifications would also remind me to check in, which helped keep it on my radar.
Q4: Yeah, after the first few weeks it became pretty automatic to check the LMS in the morning while having breakfast. I'd scroll through, see what was new, maybe watch a bit of a video or respond to a discussion post. It felt like checking social media almost—just part of my daily digital routine. Having that habit meant I stayed connected even when I wasn't doing heavy studying.
Q5: The video lectures were gold for me since I'm such a visual learner. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the diagrams and demonstrations. The discussion forums were also great because interacting with 42 different peers throughout the course gave me multiple perspectives on the material. When someone explained something differently than the lecturer, it often clicked better for me.
Q6: I'll be honest, I didn't complete a lot of the smaller tasks and assignments—my completion rate was pretty low. Some of the reading-heavy resources I'd skim or skip because walls of text just don't stick for me the way visual content does. If something felt like busywork or wasn't directly connected to what I'd be assessed on, I'd often deprioritize it.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see how it connected to the bigger picture or to the exams. Visual demonstrations, interactive elements, or discussions where I could test my understanding against others—those all felt valuable. If I walked away feeling like I actually understood something better, that was the measure of whether it was worthwhile.
Q8: Optional activities felt more like opportunities than obligations, which actually made me more likely to engage with them on my own terms. Required stuff sometimes felt like ticking boxes, whereas optional things I'd approach with genuine curiosity. But I'd also be quicker to drop optional activities if I got busy, since there was no penalty for skipping them.
Q9: Absolutely, a huge amount of my studying happened offline. I'd download the lecture slides and videos, then spend hours creating my own visual summaries and diagrams on paper or in drawing apps. I also used YouTube for supplementary explanations and had a few textbooks I'd reference. My actual learning time was way more than what the LMS could track.
Q10: When I was creating mind maps from downloaded content or working through practice problems in my notebook, none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also study with friends in person, quizzing each other and discussing concepts for hours. Some of my best learning happened in those group sessions at the library or someone's flat, completely invisible to the system.
Q11: All the time, honestly. I'd watch a video once on the LMS to download it or take screenshots of key diagrams, then I'd review those materials repeatedly offline. My phone's gallery was full of lecture screenshots that I'd flip through on the bus or before bed. One view on the system might represent five or six actual study sessions with that content.
Q12: There were a few weeks mid-semester where my extracurriculars got really demanding and my attendance dropped significantly. I was still keeping up with the core content, but I wasn't logging into the LMS as much or attending live sessions. It wasn't that I lost interest—life just got busy and I had to prioritize differently for a bit.
Q13: When I had competing responsibilities, I'd definitely use the LMS more strategically rather than comprehensively. I'd grab what I needed quickly rather than exploring everything available. Fatigue sometimes meant I'd log in, look at something, and realize I wasn't absorbing it, so I'd close it and come back later when I was fresher.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd have the LMS open while doing other things, or I'd click through materials without really processing them. There were times I'd watch a video but be thinking about something else entirely. Those sessions technically counted as engagement but weren't actually productive learning moments for me.
Q15: I definitely postponed assignments more than I should have—my assignment completion was really low even though I knew they mattered. I think because my exam performance was strong, I rationalized that the assignments weren't as critical. Looking back, I probably relied too much on my ability to perform well on exams without doing all the practice work.
Q16: Deadlines created these spikes in my LMS activity where I'd suddenly be much more engaged in the days leading up to them. I'd review discussions, rewatch videos, and check all the materials I might have glossed over. Without deadlines, my engagement was more casual and exploratory. They were definitely necessary for keeping me accountable.
Q17: When I got feedback on discussions or the few assignments I did submit, it motivated me to engage more with related content. Positive feedback especially made me want to participate more in forums. But since my assignment submission was low, I didn't get as much feedback as I probably could have, which was a missed opportunity.
Q18: The course structure gave me flexibility, which I appreciated but maybe took advantage of too much. Having materials available asynchronously meant I could engage on my own schedule, but it also meant I could skip things without immediate consequences. The discussion requirements kept me socially connected to the course even when my attendance was spotty.
Q19: The visual resources and video content made engagement easier for someone like me. What made it harder was when there were lots of text-heavy readings or when the connection between activities and assessments wasn't clear. I engaged more when I could see the direct relevance of what I was doing.
Q20: I think that could happen when students are genuinely trying to learn and understand but struggling with the specific format of assignments. Or maybe they're exploring out of interest but the assessed work doesn't align with what they find engaging. For me, I was active because I enjoyed the discussions and videos, but assignments just felt like a different kind of task I kept putting off.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me. My LMS activity wasn't always super high in terms of task completion, but I performed well because I was doing substantial work offline. I think students who are efficient learners or who use external resources effectively can absolutely succeed without their LMS showing constant activity. The platform only captures part of the picture.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns—when you're active, what you click on, how often you participate in discussions. But they miss the quality of engagement and all the offline work. They can't tell if someone really understood a video or just had it playing. For visual learners like me who transform content into personal formats, a lot of real learning is invisible.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the course material and making connections, whether that's in the LMS or not. It's about curiosity and wanting to understand, not just completing tasks. For me, engagement showed up in my peer interactions and my exam performance, even if my assignment completion didn't reflect it. It's a mental investment in actually learning.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show all the visual study materials I created, the group study sessions, or the hours I spent with downloaded content. It also wouldn't capture that I was genuinely happy and interested in the subject despite my low motivation scores—I just engaged in my own way. My distinction grade came from deep learning that happened mostly outside the trackable system.
